title: BRICKSTORM Backdoor Investigation Exercise
description: Investigate a suspected BRICKSTORM backdoor incident in a simulated environment.
outcomes: Identify BRICKSTORM Backdoor Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)
Analyze System Logs for BRICKSTORM Backdoor Activity

levels.title: Introduction to the BRICKSTORM Backdoor Incident
levels.content: Welcome to the BRICKSTORM Backdoor Investigation Exercise. Your mission is to investigate a suspected BRICKSTORM backdoor incident on a target system. The malware is known for its sophisticated Go-based backdoor, secure command and control (C2), and self-monitoring function that reinstalls or restarts if disrupted.

levels.title: Accessing the Target System
levels.cloud_content: Connect to the target system using SSH and begin your investigation.
levels.local_content: The target system (IP address: 192.168.50.5) runs Ubuntu and has an SSH service available for login. The username is cyrano, and the password is provided in Ansible.

levels.title: Identify BRICKSTORM Backdoor Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)
levels.content: Search for known indicators of compromise related to the BRICKSTORM backdoor, such as hashes, IP addresses, domains, or filenames. Use command-line tools like `grep` and `find` to search for IOCs in relevant system directories (e.g., /etc, /var/log, /home).
levels.solution:
Use the following commands to search for BRICKSTORM backdoor indicators:

1. Search for known hashes in system files:
```bash
grep -r 'hash_value' /etc/* /var/log/* /home/*
```
Replace `hash_value` with the appropriate hash value from the MISP Event.

2. Check system logs for unusual network connections or commands:
```bash
grep -i 'unusual_keyword' /var/log/syslog /var/log/auth.log
```
Replace `unusual_keyword` with a keyword that might indicate BRICKSTORM backdoor activity.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: Look for known BRICKSTORM backdoor hashes in system files.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: Check system logs for unusual network connections or commands.

levels.title: Analyze System Logs for BRICKSTORM Backdoor Activity
levels.content: Examine system logs (e.g., /var/log/syslog, /var/log/auth.log) to identify any suspicious activity related to the BRICKSTORM backdoor.
levels.solution:
Use command-line tools like `grep` and `less` to search for specific keywords or patterns in system logs.

1. Search for unusual network connections:
```bash
grep -i 'unusual_keyword' /var/log/syslog
```
Replace `unusual_keyword` with a keyword that might indicate BRICKSTORM backdoor activity.

2. Check for suspicious commands:
```bash
grep -i 'suspicious_command' /var/log/auth.log
```
Replace `suspicious_command` with a command that might be associated with the BRICKSTORM backdoor.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: Check system logs for unusual network connections or commands.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: Look for suspicious activity in system logs.

levels.title: Gather Evidence and Document Findings
levels.content: Collect evidence of the BRICKSTORM backdoor activity and document your findings in a report.
levels.solution:
Use command-line tools like `cp`, `mv`, and text editors (e.g., nano, vim) to gather relevant files and create a report detailing your investigation.

1. Gather evidence:
```bash
mv /path/to/evidence /report_directory/
```
Replace `/path/to/evidence` with the path to the evidence file(s) you found and `/report_directory/` with the directory where you want to store your report.

2. Create a report:
```bash
nano /report_directory/report.txt
```
Open the report file in nano or another text editor and document your findings.

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: Gather evidence of the BRICKSTORM backdoor activity.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: Document your findings in a report.
